fr:title.text: Lecture 6: Using matrices to compare logics

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Lecture 6: Using matrices to compare logics

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Last lecture, we saw what a matrix is, and how, once we have a matrix, we can use that matrix to determine a logic. This time, we'll look at one of the ways matrices can be used to compare logics. In particular, we'll use the matrices from last time to compare classical logic and the logics K3, LP, and FDE in terms of strength.

fr:title.text: A first example: classical logic and K3

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: A first example: classical logic and K3

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Let's start with an example, before we zoom out to look at the big idea. For this example, I'll walk you through some reasoning that compares K3 to classical logic in terms of strength.
Before we start, you might want to ask yourself: is K3 stronger than classical logic? is it weaker? both stronger and weaker? neither? Pause and think; what do you expect? How sure are you?
Okay, let's find out!

When we're asking about strength and weakness, we're asking about which arguments are in these logics. And there's a sense in which we already know exactly which arguments are in these logics: an argument is in classical logic iff it has no counterexamples in the classical matrix; and an argument is in K3 iff it has no counterexamples in the K3 matrix.

Our job here, then, is to draw these two ideas together, to make them comparable. What relationships can we find between classical counterexamples and K3 counterexamples? Here it can help to drill down to a more basic level: classical counterexamples are a certain kind of Boolean valuation, and K3 counterexamples are a certain kind of strong Kleene valuation. So what relationships can we find between Boolean valuations and strong Kleene valuations?

fr:title.text: Boolean valuations and strong Kleene valuations

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Boolean valuations and strong Kleene valuations

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Well, imagine we have some Boolean valuation v. This must assign either the value \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} or the value \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} to every atomic sentence in the language, and then it must accord with the Boolean tables for every connective.

What I want to point out now, though, is that this is actually enough for v to also be a strong Kleene valuation. To see this, start with atomic sentences. The strong Kleene structure has the three values \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, and \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}, and any way of assigning these values to the atomic sentences results in a strong Kleene valuation. But if we take our Boolean valuation v, we can see that it does exactly this! It just does it in a way that doesn't use the value \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} for any atomic sentence.

Assigning legit values to the atomic sentences, of course, is not enough for v to be a strong Kleene valuation. It also has to assign values to compound sentences in a way that accords with the strong Kleene tables. But have a look at the Boolean tables compared to the strong Kleene tables, connective by connective.
For example, let's look at the tables for conjunction:

Note that the Boolean table here lives inside the strong Kleene one. That is, if we're calculating the value of a conjunction in accordance with the strong Kleene table, if both components take values from \{\mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}\}, then the result will be exactly the same as if we were calculating the value in accordance with the Boolean table instead.

And it's not just conjunction: this same relationship holds for all our other connectives. As long as we start from just \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, we always get the same results in the strong Kleene structure that we get in the Boolean structure. The difference between the structures only comes up when some sentence gets the value \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}: this can't happen in the Boolean structure. But as long as all the atomic sentences get values \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, then there can't be any divergence.
This means that every Boolean valuation is at the same time a strong Kleene valuation. That's an important link between the two structures.
With that link in hand, we're ready to return to counterexamples, to see if we can find a connection between classical counterexamples and K3 counterexamples.

Start by thinking: what is a classical counterexample to an argument \Gamma \;/\; A? It's a Boolean valuation v where:
for every B in \Gamma , we have v(B) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, and
we also have v(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}.

Now, we've just seen that every Boolean valuation is also a strong Kleene valuation. So imagine that we have some classical counterexample to some argument \Gamma \;/\; A. Is it a K3 counterexample to \Gamma \;/\; A as well? Since it's a classical counterexample, it's a Boolean valuation, so it's also a strong Kleene valuation, which is a start. What does a strong Kleene valuation need to be like to be a K3 counterexample to this argument? It needs to give:
for every B in \Gamma , we have either v(B) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, and
we also have either v(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} or v(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}.

If v is a classical counterexample to \Gamma \;/\; A, then, it is also a K3 counterexample to that same argument: it brings all the premises to the value \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, which is designated in both matrices, and it brings the conclusion to the value \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, which is undesignated in both matrices. So every classical counterexample to an argument is at the same time a K3 counterexample to that argument as well.

fr:title.text: What does this tell us about strength?

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: What does this tell us about strength?

fr:mainmatter.html:p: That's the key fact that we need. Getting there depended on a few things: we had to think in detail about all the tables for all the connectives in both structures, as well as about which values count as designated in both matrices. But as a result of looking through that detail, we've now got something we can use.

Take any argument that is invalid in classical logic. Since it is invalid in classical logic, it must have a classical counterexample. But we have just seen that that means that it also has a K3 counterexample: the very same valuation that is its classical counterexample. So that means that it must also be invalid in K3.

So any argument invalid in classical logic is also invalid in K3. That means that any argument valid in K3 must also be valid in classical logic: if it wasn't valid in classical logic, we've seen that it would have to also be invalid in K3. That is, K3 is a subset of classical logic: K3 is weaker than classical logic.

There's no magic tricks here! This is just a matter of moving slowly and methodically through the ideas we have so far, and seeing how they fit together. But the result gives us a discovery: we now know that K3 is weaker than classical logic.

Moreover, you already know from last time that K3 is not stronger than classical logic, since the argument \;/\; p \lor \neg p is valid in classical logic but not in K3. So we've now got the full story about the relationship between these two logics in terms of strength: K3 is weaker than classical logic, but not vice versa.

That's the main idea I want to look at in this lecture: how we can move from one kind of counterexample to another, and use that sort of motion to compare two logics to each other in terms of strength.

As you've seen, in the above example we got to a place where, whenever we have a counterexample to an argument in one matrix, then that is also a counterexample to that same argument in another matrix. Whenever that situation obtains, we can be sure that the logic determined by the second matrix is weaker than the logic determined by the first.

But sometimes it's useful to be slightly more general. It's not really important to this reasoning that the counterexample in the second matrix be the exact same valuation as the counterexample in the first matrix. All we're doing is showing that an argument has a counterexample, so that it comes out invalid.

So really, the idea here is to get to a place where, whenever we have a counterexample to an argument in one matrix, then we can be sure that there is some counterexample to that same argument in another matrix. That's enough to be sure that the logic determined by the second matrix is weaker than the logic determined by the first.

In our above example, we could be sure that there was some K3 counterexample, because it just was the Boolean counterexample. But more generally, we might use one kind of counterexample to find a different kind. The next example we'll look at is of that sort.

fr:title.text: A second example: LP and FDE

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: A second example: LP and FDE

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Let's take a look at the logics LP and FDE, to try to compare these to each other using the same technique. As before, we should start by thinking about the valuations: how do strong Kleene valuations relate to Dunn-Belnap valuations? Then we can turn to thinking about counterexamples, just as before.

fr:title.text: Strong Kleene valuations and Dunn-Belnap valuations

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Strong Kleene valuations and Dunn-Belnap valuations

fr:mainmatter.html:p: When it comes to valuations, the relationship is more complex this time: we don't have simply that every strong Kleene valation is a Dunn-Belnap valuation or vice versa. The reason is simple: there are strong Kleene valuations that use the value \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}, which is not a value in the Dunn-Belnap structure, and there are Dunn-Belnap valuations that use the values \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}, which are not values in the strong Kleene structure.
So to find a relationship between these structures, we need to be a bit more clever. It helps to start by looking at the connective tables and seeing what we see. Here are the conjunction tables:

One thing that you might notice---and if you didn't, let me now draw your attention to it---is that the Boolean table for conjunction is contained in both of these. Whenever we use the values \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} exclusively, these tables both agree with the Boolean table, and so they agree with each other. The same is true for all our other connectives as well: as long as we have only the values \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} in play, all three of these structures match perfectly.

But that's not enough yet: we need some way to compare the remaining values as well. I think at this point that there are three natural things to try. It will turn out that not all of them work! But let's step through each possibility in turn.

Suppose we have a strong Kleene valuation v. That valuation itself need not be a Dunn-Belnap valuation, as noted above, since it might assign the value \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} to some atomic sentence. But perhaps we can use it to find a particular Dunn-Belnap valuation u that we can work with.

Here's one way we might do it. For every atomic sentence A, if v(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} or v(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, then we let u(A) = v(A). That is, if we're already using a value that's present in the Dunn-Belnap structure, we leave it alone and let the Dunn-Belnap valuation u match our strong Kleene valuation v for that atomic sentence. On the other hand, if v(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}, then we let u(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}.

Now we've set up a way where, given any strong Kleene valuation, we can determine a Dunn-Belnap valuation, and we know how they're related for atomic sentences, because of how we've set them up: they match on values \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, and they also kind of match on other values, except that wherever the strong Kleene valuation has \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} the Dunn-Belnap valuation has \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}, and vice versa.

What about compound sentences, though? Unlike atomic sentences, we can't just declare that these work however we want; we have tables to deal with in the two structures. So we need to look into these tables. As an example, let me reproduce the negation tables here:

As I pointed out before, here we have agreement between these tables on what they do with \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. But what about \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}? Here, too, we have a certain kind of "agreement": each of these values gets taken by negation to itself. So if v(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} then v(\neg A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}, and if u(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} then u(\neg A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}.

Here it helps to be a bit more precise in a certain kind of ritualised way. (With all our symbols flying around, this is probably the class where you're most likely to accidentally summon a demon anyhow; let's lean into it!) The ritualised reasoning I want to introduce here is called reasoning by induction.

Actually, there are at least two different kinds of reasoning that get called reasoning by induction, and they are very much not the same! One is the kind that plays a role in the so-called "problem of induction"; that's not what I'm talking about here. The other is one that's sometimes called "mathematical induction" or "structural induction", and that's what we are doing.

The core of the idea is this: suppose we want to securely demonstrate that all formulas are such-and-such a way. The idea of induction doesn't depend on what we're trying to show about all formulas, so for now let's just say we want to show that all formulas are happy.

We can use what we know about how formulas are built up to systematically think about all formulas, and make sure we haven't left any of them out. Since there are more than six or seven formulas, we need some kind of system! The structure of formulas themselves is a very useful system much of the time, and it's what I'll use here.

We want to start, then, with the atomic sentences, showing that they are all happy. In practice, how we do this will depend on what it is we're really showing about all sentences; induction provides us no help here. But suppose we can do it: we've shown that all atomic sentences are happy.

What remains is all the compound sentences, and this is where induction really gives us its power. All we need to do here is to show that if all the components of a compound sentence are happy, then the compound sentence itself is also happy. That is, in showing that the compound sentence is happy, we can help ourselves to the assumption that all its components are happy.
This is important enough that it's handy to have a word for it:

A connective in a language preserves a property of sentences iff: whenever that connective is the main connective of a sentence, if all the components of that sentence have the property, then so does the sentence itself.
So what we're aiming to do is to show that all the connectives preserve the property of happiness.

Think about why this works: suppose it's true that all atomic sentences are happy, and that all the connectives preserve happiness. How, then, could there ever be a sentence that wasn't happy? How could it be built? We have to start from atomic sentences that are happy, and build up using connectives that can't ever bring us from happy sentences to not-happy ones. So any sentence we can manage to build in this way must be happy---but that's all the sentences!

If we can show the two parts of what we need, then:
that all atomic sentences are happy, and
that all connectives preserve happiness, then we've shown "by induction" that all sentences are happy.

Okay, back to what we're doing. Here, what we want to show about all formulas is a bit complex. But really, it's exactly what we've set up to be true for all atomic sentences---we want to show that that's true of all sentences. That is, we're going to reason by induction, but instead of showing that all formulas are "happy", we're going to show that all formulas A are such that:
if v(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} or v(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, then u(A) = v(A), and
if v(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}, then u(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}.

To show by induction that all formulas have this complex property, we start by showing that all atomic sentences have it. Here our job is easy: the way we set up the Dunn-Belnap valuation u, it does exactly this for all atomic sentences! So the first half of what we need is already done.

The second half is to work connective by connective, and see that each connective preserves this property. Consider negation, for example. We want to see that, whenever any sentence A has this property, then \neg A also has the property. So imagine that we have some sentence A with the property. Then one of three things is the case: either
v(A) = u(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, or
v(A) = u(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, or
v(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} and u(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}.

Now, looking at our negation tables, we can see that these three cases give us, respectively:
v(
eg A) = u(
eg A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, or
v(
eg A) = u(
eg A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, or
v(
eg A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} and u(
eg A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}. And in all three of those cases, we can see that 
eg A has the property we're concerned with. So 
eg preserves this property.

To finish the job, we need to think about every connective in turn. I won't go through them all here, since it gets pretty repetitive. But I will look at one binary and one nullary connective, so that you can see how this reasoning goes at various different arities. You should read this looking for the general pattern of reasoning. The details are important, but they are also just details. Make sure you see the big picture too! That way you can use this technique to answer your own questions (and puzzles).

Let's look at a binary connective: \lor . We want to show that \lor preserves the property. So consider any sentence A \lor B, and suppose that both A and B have the property; we want to show that A \lor B has it as well.

This gives us nine possibilities to think through, as each of A and B gives three possibilities: either it has value \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} in both v and u, or it has \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} in both v and u, or it has \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} in v and \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} in u. Since these are independent, and we have two components to consider, there are nine overall possibilities. These possibilities are nicely summed up in the strong Kleene table for disjunction, which also tells us v(A \lor B) for each of these possibilities:
It remains, then, to check u(A \lor B) on these same nine possibilities. Here we can start from the Dunn-Belnap table for disjunction, since u is a Dunn-Belnap valuation:

Notice, though, that we can ignore the row and the column headed by "\mathsf {\mathbf {B}}". After all, we're supposing that both A and B have the property, and that means that both u(A) and u(B) must not be \mathsf {\mathbf {B}}.
Look at it, then, more like this:

And now note, if you focus just on what's not faded out, how similar this looks to the strong Kleene table. You can use these tables to walk through all nine possibilities, and see that in each case, indeed A \lor B has the property.

That is, suppose v(A) = v(B) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}; then we are in the top left corner of the strong Kleene table. Since both A and B have the property, this means that u(A) = u(B) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, so we are also in the top left corner of the Dunn-Belnap table. Comparing those two top-left corners, we see that v(A \lor B) = u(A \lor B) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}; so in this case A \lor B has the property.

That preceding paragraph is one of the nine cases that need considering. Take a moment to walk through at least a few of the other cases at that level of detail, making sure you see how every sinew of the reasoning connects. If you do it properly, you'll see that it really does work!

As is usual, the situation with our nullary connectives is maybe a little weirder-feeling, but in fact much simpler. Let's look at the case of \top . In both strong Kleene and Dunn-Belnap valuations, this sentence always receives the value \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}; so it has the property. That's it!

Let's take stock. We've seen that, for any strong Kleene valuation v, we can set up a Dunn-Belnap valuation u so that every atomic sentence A has the following property:
if v(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, then u(A) = v(A), and
if v(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}, then u(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}.
We've also seen that every connective preserves this property. So we can conclude by induction that the property holds of every sentence, not just the atomic ones.

This doesn't show that strong Kleene valuations are Dunn-Belnap valuations. But it does show a way to translate any strong Kleene valuation into an importantly-related Dunn-Belnap valuation: leave all the \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} values alone, and change every \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} to \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}. For now, put a pin in that; we'll come back to it in a moment. We've got other possibilities to consider first!

Again, suppose we have a strong Kleene valuation v. Let's try a different tack this time, to see if we can treat the value \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} like \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} instead. That is, let's set up a Dunn-Belnap valuation t so that, for every atomic sentence A, we have:
if v(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} then t(A) = v(A), and
if v(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} then t(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {B}}.

This certainly determines some Dunn-Belnap valuation; but as before, we can't say much about the result yet. It would be convenient if the above property turns out to hold for all sentences, not just the atomic ones. As before, we can attempt to show this via induction. For convenience, let's call this property "the \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} / \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} property"; note that it's a different property from the one we looked at before. (Note, though, that there's nothing particularly important about this property in its own right; we're just using it for a moment, to reason by induction.) I'll go through the reasoning a bit quicker this time, so you can see the pattern again.
Let's start with \neg again. Here are the strong Kleene and Dunn-Belnap tables for \neg :

Now, we want to show that if any sentence A has the property, then 
eg A has it as well. There are three possibilities:
v(
eg A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, or
v(
eg A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, or
v(
eg A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}.

This shows that, no matter what v(
eg A) is, t(
eg A) is related to it in the right way. So as long as A has the \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} / \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} property, then 
eg A has it as well; this shows that 
eg preserves the \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} / \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} property.

Again, I'll only look at one binary connective, since they all follow the same pattern of reasoning; this time I'll pick \rightarrow for the sake of variety. Here are the strong Kleene and Dunn-Belnap tables for \rightarrow :

We want to show that \rightarrow preserves the \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} / \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} property, that whenever both A and B have the property, then A \rightarrow B has it as well. But if indeed both A and B have the property, then we know that u(A) and u(B) can't be \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}, so we can set aside those rows and columns of the Dunn-Belnap table:

And now you can compare that to the strong Kleene table to see that these tables match on \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, and that they use \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} in the same places as each other. So \rightarrow too preserves the \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} / \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} property.

To conclude that every sentence has the \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} / \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} property, we need to know that every connective, not just \neg and \rightarrow , preserves the property. But here I'll just tell you: they do.

You should know now, though, how you would go about discovering this; what you would be looking at about each connective in turn in order to detect whether that connective does or does not preserve the property.

Since every connective preserves the \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} / \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} property, and every atomic sentence has the property, we can conclude that every sentence has the property. This lets us know that, given any strong Kleene valuation v, we can generate a Dunn-Belnap valuation t that matches v on all \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} values, and that uses \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} wherever v uses \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}.

Combining this with what we learned before, we now know that every strong Kleene valuation can be turned into a Dunn-Belnap valuation two different ways: we can treat \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} everywhere as \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}, or we can treat it everywhere as \mathsf {\mathbf {B}}. Again, put a pin in that for now.

fr:title.text: Can we treat both \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} as \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}?

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Can we treat both \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} as \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}?

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A third thing that we might think about is coming back the other way. We've seen two different ways to get Dunn-Belnap valuations from a strong Kleene valuation. But suppose we have a Dunn-Belnap valuation instead; can we use that to reach a strong Kleene valuation in a useful way?

Spoiler: this is the idea I want to explore that won't work out, so that you can see up close what it looks like when this doesn't go smoothly. This is important: when you're doing reasoning like this to explore a question, you can't know in advance how things will go. That's why you're doing the reasoning: to find out! We've seen two cases where it does work, but those are very special. More usual would be a case like this, where it doesn't work. Let's dive in!

Suppose we begin from some Dunn-Belnap valuation v, and we want to generate a strong Kleene valuation s. One natural thought, based roughly on what we've just looked at, might be to leave \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} as they are, and to treat both \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} as \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}. That is, let's think about the strong Kleene valuation s such that for all atomic sentences A, we have:
if v(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, then s(A) = v(A), and
if v(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}, then s(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}.

Since valuations can do whatever they like with atomic sentences, there surely is such a strong Kleene valuation s. Again, it's handy to have a name for the property we're looking at, the one we set up for all atomic sentences, so that we can explore whether it applies to all sentences. Let's call it the "\mathsf {\mathbf {B}} / \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} / \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} property".
So now we want to see whether all connectives preserve the \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} / \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} / \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} property.
Let's start with our tables for negation:
Now, supposing that A has the \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} / \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} / \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} property, can we show that \neg A does as well?

Here there are four possibilities to consider:
v(\neg A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, or
v(\neg A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {B}}, or
v(\neg A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}, or
v(\neg A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}.
This checks out! So indeed, if A has the \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} / \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} / \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} property, then \neg A does as well; this means that \neg preserves this property.
Let's move on to a binary connective. Here are the tables for \land in the two structures we're thinking about:
Again, we want to show that whenever sentences A and B have the property, then A \land B does as well.

This time, assuming that A and B have the property doesn't let us ignore any parts of these tables, though; we've got to consider all possibilities, since we're starting from any Dunn-Belnap valuation v. Instead, we want to think of what we're trying as like "smooshing" the two values \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} down into the one value \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}, while leaving \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} alone. So you might even look at the Dunn-Belnap table here like this:

But now we can see a problem. Looking to the strong Kleene table, we can see three internal cells with the value \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}. These are the values s gives to A \land B when either A or B has the value \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}, and neither of them has the value \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} That is, when either s(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} or s(B) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} and both s(A) and s(B) are not \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, then s(A \land B) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}.

Now look to the Dunn-Belnap table. When s(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}, then v(A) must be either \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}. And similarly, when s(B) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}, then v(B) must be either \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}. (Remember, we're supposing that A and B have the \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} / \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} / \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} property.) Similarly, if both s(A) and s(B) are not \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, then both v(A) and v(B) must not be \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} either. So we have eight possibilities to consider; these are the distinguished cells in the table.

For A \land B to have the property, we would need to see only \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} in those distinguished cells. But there are two \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}s in there! This gives us the clue we need to show that \land does not preserve the \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} / \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} / \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} property.

It's time to fill in a concrete case. Suppose our initial Dunn-Belnap valuation v is such that v(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} and v(q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}. Then v(p \land q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. But the strong Kleene valuation s we've determined from v has s(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} and s(q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}, so s(p \land q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}. The \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} / \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} / \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} property fails for p \land q. But we set up s so that the property would hold for p and q; so we can see that \land does not preserve the property.

So, given a Dunn-Belnap valuation v, we can indeed reach a strong Kleene valuation s as specified. But this strong Kleene valuation is not related to v in any particularly convenient way, at least as far as we've seen. The attempt to extend the \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} / \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} / \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} property from atomic sentences to all sentences has failed.

Moreover, it's not just like we tried to show this and came up empty: we even found a particular Dunn-Belnap valuation v where the \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} / \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} / \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} property does not hold of p \land q. So there's no point in continuing to try to show that this property holds of all sentences; we know it doesn't.

Here's another way to look at what we've shown: given a Dunn-Belnap valuation v, we might set up a function r from sentences to strong Kleene values that matches v on \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, and collapses both of the values \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} to the single value \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}. What we've just shown, though, is that this function r won't generally be a strong Kleene valuation! It will match our strong Kleene valuation s on atomic sentences, but not on all sentences. So there isn't much we could do with r to connect Dunn-Belnap valuations to the strong Kleene structure.

Unfortunately, that doesn't really tell us much else. Is our strong Kleene valuation s related to v in some other way worth exploring? For all I've said here, maybe it is and maybe it isn't. Or is there some other way to take a Dunn-Belnap valuation and generate a strong Kleene valuation that's usefully related to it? For all I've said here, maybe there is and maybe there isn't.
That's just life, I'm afraid: it's nice when things work out, but it's not always so!

Let's take stock now. We've explored three different possibilities for connecting strong Kleene valuations to Dunn-Belnap valuations. Two of these ways worked out: we can take any strong Kleene valuation and replace all \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}s with \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}s to reach a Dunn-Belnap valuation, or we can replace them all with \mathsf {\mathbf {B}}s to reach a different Dunn-Belnap valuation. The third way didn't work out: if we take a Dunn-Belnap valuation, collapsing all \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} values to the single value \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} doesn't generally give us a strong Kleene valuation at all. Going forward, let's forget about what didn't work, and focus on what did.

This is all in the service, remember, of using our matrices to compare the logics LP and FDE. Now that we've got a sense of how things work out with valuations, let's turn to the matrices for LP and FDE, so that we can think about these.

We know that our methods for turning strong Kleene valuations into Dunn-Belnap valuations are the methods that work, so we'll focus on that direction. Suppose we have a strong Kleene valuation v that is an LP counterexample to an argument \Gamma \;/\; A. That is:
for every sentence B in \Gamma , v(B) is either \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}, and
v(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}.

What can we do with this? What we'd like to do is to create an FDE counterexample to the same argument. If we can do that, we'll have shown that FDE is weaker than LP, by the same reasoning we used to show that K3 is weaker than classical logic.

And we have two tools we can try. We know that there is a Dunn-Belnap valuation u that is just like v except that it uses \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} wherever v uses \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}, and there is a Dunn-Belnap valuation t that is just like v except that it uses \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} instead.

Think about this valuation t in particular. Because of what we know about v, we know that:
for every sentence B in \Gamma , t(B) is either \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {B}}, and
t(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}.

But that's enough for t to be an FDE counterexample to the argument \Gamma \;/\; A: the values \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} are designated in the matrix for FDE, and the value \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} is not. (The value \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} also is not, but that doesn't matter here, since t doesn't assign \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} to any sentence.)

So we've got another instance of the overall pattern described above: whenever there is an LP counterexample to any argument, there is also an FDE counterexample to that same argument. So if an argument is invalid in LP, it must also be invalid in FDE. This guarantees that FDE is weaker than LP.

fr:title.text: A third example for free: K3 and FDE

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: A third example for free: K3 and FDE

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Given the above, though, we also get some useful information about K3 and FDE. Here, we've already done almost all the work; we just need to put together the pieces in the right way.

Suppose we have some strong Kleene valuation v that is a K3 counterexample to an argument \Gamma \;/\; A. That is:
for every sentence B in \Gamma , v(B) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, and
v(A) is either \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}.

As we've seen, we can use v to reach a Dunn-Belnap valuation u that matches v on values \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, and that uses \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} wherever v uses \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}. So:
for every sentence B in \Gamma , u(B) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, and
u(A) is either \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}.
And that's enough for u to be an FDE counterexample to \Gamma \;/\; A. So FDE is also weaker than K3.

Besides giving us another example of the key reasoning pattern for this lecture, this also gives another lesson: when we work out the solution to a puzzle systematically, by trying different approaches and keeping track of which ones work, we often end up solving more than just the original puzzle. Here we set out to inquire about LP and FDE; we tried two different ways of transforming strong Kleene valuations into Dunn-Belnap valuations, and they both worked. One of those ways turned out to be the key to answering the original question about LP and FDE, but the other way wasn't useless at all: it is the key to answering this different question about K3 and FDE.
Moreover, we already saw that FDE is distinct from both LP and K3. So now we know just how these logics relate in terms of strength: FDE is weaker than both LP and K3, and not vice versa.

We're now getting to the real work of logic. Note that there's only one new definition in this lecture! I think this is the first time we've had so few. We're rather working with all the definitions we've built up over the preceding weeks, and exploring some of the texture that results.

The headlines this time are the strength facts we've shown: K3 is weaker than classical logic, FDE is weaker than LP, FDE is weaker than K3. (We haven't asked about LP and classical logic, but you now have enough to be able to figure this one out!)

More important than these headlines, though, is the techniques we developed on the way there. First, there is the key way we used matrices: when we have two logics given via matrices, if we can transform any counterexample to an argument in one of these matrices into a counterexample to the same argument in the other matrix, then we can conclude that the second logic is weaker than the first.

And second, there is the technique of reasoning by induction that is key for applying this technique to matrices built on different structures. Here, if we want to show that any property holds of all sentences in our language, we show that it holds for all atomic sentences and that all connectives preserve the property. Since all sentences are built from atomics via connectives, this is enough to ensure that every sentence has the property.

html:ul.html:li:
Section A: Preliminaries
Lecture 1: Introduction and languages
Lecture 2: Functions and substitutions
Lecture 3: Sets, arguments, and logics

Section B: Structures and matrices
Lecture 4: Structures
Lecture 5: Matrices and counterexamples
Lecture 6: Using matrices to compare logics
[Lecture 7: The limits of matrices]

Section C: Exploring intuitionistic logic
Lecture 8: Natural deduction
Lecture 9: Intuitionistic logic
Lecture 10: Intuitionistic models
Lecture 11: Harmony

fr:title.text: Counterexample in a matrix, logic determined by a matrix

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Counterexample in a matrix, logic determined by a matrix

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
Given a matrix with structure S and set \Delta of designated values, a valuation v in the structure S is a counterexample to an argument \Gamma \;/\; A in that matrix iff both:
for every B in \Gamma , v(B) is in \Delta , and
v(A) is not in \Delta
The logic determined by a matrix is the logic that contains an argument iff that argument has no counterexamples in the matrix.
A matrix has two parts: first, a structure; and second, a subset of the structure's set of values. The values in this subset are called the designated values of the matrix.
